experience_level: Experienced gym-goer with significant ad hoc training history but no structured programming experience
injuries: Rotator cuffs strain easily — avoid heavy overhead loading and vulnerable shoulder positions
equipment: Apartment building gym with free weights, barbells, squat racks, reformer machine, and pool
schedule: 3-4 days per week, 1 hour per session
preferences: Enjoys progressive overload and lifting heavier over time; dislikes running; open to pool-based cardio

goals.text: Build muscle size and look bigger
Improve cardio fitness and achieve above-average VO2 max
General wellbeing and mood benefits from exercise

cardio_history: Self-assessed at or below average cardio fitness; has army background so some prior fitness base; observed max HR around 160-170 bpm during runs

strength.notes: 80 kg × 4 reps → Epley 1RM ≈ 87 kg. Got 4 reps instead of the target 5, indicating 80 kg was slightly above true 5RM — conservative estimate warranted. Working weight set at ~83% of estimated 1RM for a solid 3×5 starting point. Good squat mobility observed (flat heels, full depth, no discomfort), so depth is not a limiter. Squat-to-deadlift ratio is typical; monitor that gap doesn't widen excessively as loads climb.

90 kg × 5 reps → Epley 1RM ≈ 103 kg. Clean set of 5, making this the most reliable data point of the session. Working weight set at ~82.5% for 3×5 linear progression. Deadlift is the relative strength leader — expected, but worth ensuring squat development keeps pace over the coming months.

60 kg × 5 reps → Epley 1RM ≈ 67 kg. Protocol explicitly avoided true failure given rotator cuff sensitivity, so actual capacity may be marginally higher — conservative estimate stands. Working weight at ~82% of 1RM. Elbows-in cue (45–60° from torso) is mandatory every session. Any anterior shoulder discomfort at or below this weight is a hard stop. No wide-grip, no heavy barbell overhead pressing until shoulder complex is formally assessed and conditioned.

40 reps to failure after bench press is an excellent result — well above the population median for a 30-year-old male, especially performed post-bench. Indicates strong relative upper-body pushing endurance and a favorable bodyweight-to-strength ratio at 61.7 kg. No 1RM applicable. Use as a muscular endurance benchmark; weighted or archer push-up variations make good accessory volume options.

cardio.level: Moderate — low-to-mid Zone 2 aerobic base, meaningful room to grow toward stated goals
cardio.details: Swam 550 m in 20 minutes at an average HR of 125 bpm and RPE 6/10. Using a max HR of 187 bpm, 125 bpm represents approximately 67% HRmax — which sits at the lower end of his Zone 2 band (112–131 bpm) and is actually well-placed aerobically, suggesting the swim pace was reasonably calibrated. RPE 6 is consistent with a sustainable aerobic effort. 550 m in 20 min (1,650 m/hr) reflects a moderate swim pace for a recreational swimmer. Resting HR of ~51 bpm and HRV averaging ~56 ms over the past week indicate reasonable autonomic health and a decent underlying base, likely from army background. Estimated VO2 max sits in the 40–44 ml/kg/min range — average for a 30-year-old male (age-group 'above average' threshold is roughly 47–50 ml/kg/min). Cardio goals (5k, sub-12-min 1.5-mile run, above-average VO2 max) are ambitious but very achievable with consistent structured work over 6–12 months.

baseline.core: Above average endurance — 90-second forearm plank is a solid result, comfortably above the general population median of ~60 seconds and in the 'good' category for a 30-year-old male. Core stability appears functional and sufficient to support barbell compound work at current loads. Not yet exceptional; progressing toward 2+ minutes and introducing anti-rotation work (Pallof press, dead bugs, ab wheel rollouts) will pay dividends as lifting loads scale up.
baseline.mobility: Lower-body mobility is a genuine asset. Full-depth bodyweight squat with flat heel contact, no reported compensation, and no discomfort — an unrestricted movement pattern that many lifters lack. No ankle, hip, or thoracic limitations observed in the squat plane. Rotator cuff sensitivity (pre-existing) remains the primary constraint: internal/external rotation range and impingement thresholds have not been formally tested here, but must be respected consistently in programming — no heavy barbell overhead pressing, no wide-flare bench technique. No wrist discomfort noted during push-ups. Overall: lower-body mobility is good to excellent; the shoulder complex is the single area requiring ongoing conservative management.
baseline.summary: You're coming in with a genuinely solid foundation — squat and deadlift numbers are respectable for someone without structured programming, your push-up endurance is well above average, and your hip mobility is a real asset that many lifters spend months trying to build. Cardio is the honest growth area, sitting at average for your age group, but your resting HR and HRV suggest your body is primed to adapt quickly with the right stimulus. The rotator cuff sensitivity is a constraint to respect consistently, not a ceiling — it shapes the route rather than the destination, and there are plenty of effective ways to build an impressive upper body within those limits. Every number here gives us a clear before-picture, and 12 weeks of structured work will make the difference visible.
baseline.programming_notes: PRIMARY GOALS: hypertrophy and strength via progressive overload (main priority); aerobic base development toward 5k and sub-12-min 1.5-mile run (secondary). Client dislikes running — lean heavily on pool cardio initially; introduce run-walk intervals only after 6–8 weeks once he has expressed readiness. STRUCTURE: 3–4 days/week, ~60 min/session. Recommended split: 3-day full-body or upper/lower alternating; on a 4th day, add a dedicated pool cardio session. STRENGTH — LINEAR PROGRESSION PHASE (weeks 1–8): Start squat 72.5 kg 3×5, deadlift 85 kg 3×5, bench 55 kg 3×5. Add 2.5 kg to upper body lifts and 5 kg to lower body lifts each session while form holds. Expect linear progression to stall around weeks 6–10 — transition to intermediate programming (e.g., Texas Method or 3-day hypertrophy block) at that point. SHOULDER PROTOCOL — NON-NEGOTIABLE: No barbell overhead press. No heavy dumbbell overhead press initially. Replace vertical pressing with landmine press, machine shoulder press (neutral grip, controlled ROM), or Arnold press at light-to-moderate loads. Include face pulls or band pull-aparts as shoulder prehab at the start of every upper-body session (3×15–20). Cable lateral raises preferred over heavy dumbbell laterals for rotator cuff safety. HYPERTROPHY ACCESSORIES (after main compounds, 2–3 exercises, 3×8–12): Lower days — Romanian deadlift, leg press, leg curl, goblet squat. Upper days — dumbbell row, incline DB press (moderate angle, elbows in), cable row, lat pulldown, dips only if shoulder-safe and tested conservatively. Push-up variations (archer, weighted vest) as supplementary chest/tricep volume. CORE PROGRESSION: Week 1–4: plank 3×60–75s, dead bugs 3×10 each side, Pallof press 3×12. Week 5–8: plank toward 2 min, add ab wheel rollouts, side plank for lateral stability. CARDIO: 1–2 pool sessions/week. Zone 2 target HR: 112–131 bpm (he was at 125 bpm in the test — good zone, just needs consistency and volume). Start with 2×20-min continuous swims per week; build to 30–35 min by week 8. Every 4 weeks retest 20-min swim distance to track aerobic progress. Run-walk introduction (if/when ready): 1 min run / 2 min walk × 8 rounds, 2×/week, progressing ratio gradually. RETESTING: Squat/deadlift/bench 5RM at weeks 8 and 16. Plank and 20-min swim at week 8. UNITS: all weights in kg. SLEEP NOTE: Averaging ~6.9 hrs/night against a baseline of ~7.1 hrs — slightly below optimal for recovery and hypertrophy. Worth flagging gently; not alarming but worth monitoring if fatigue accumulates during progressive overload phases.

raw_results.name: Barbell Back Squat — Heavy Set of 5
raw_results.protocol: Warm up with 5 minutes of light movement (leg swings, bodyweight squats). Then perform: 1×8 at an empty bar, rest 90s; 1×5 at ~50% of your expected working weight, rest 90s; 1×3 at ~70%, rest 2min; 1×1 at ~85%, rest 2min. Then attempt a challenging but clean set of 5 reps — this should be a weight where rep 5 is genuinely hard but form stays solid. Bar stays on mid-to-upper traps (low bar also fine), no overhead load concern here. Rest fully before each build-up set. Stop if form breaks down.
raw_results.record_label: Weight (kg or lb) × reps completed in your best set of 5

raw_results.name: Barbell Conventional Deadlift — Heavy Set of 5
raw_results.protocol: After squats, rest 5 minutes. Warm up the deadlift with: 1×5 at 50% of your expected working weight, rest 90s; 1×3 at ~70%, rest 2min; 1×1 at ~85%, rest 2min. Then do your challenging set of 5. Keep a neutral spine throughout; this is a hinge pattern, not a squat. No shoulder vulnerability here — focus on hip drive and lat engagement. Record the heaviest set of 5 you complete with good form.
raw_results.record_label: Weight (kg or lb) × reps completed in your best set of 5

raw_results.name: Barbell Bench Press — Comfortable Heavy Set of 5
raw_results.protocol: Given rotator cuff sensitivity, use a moderate grip width (just outside shoulder width), keep elbows at roughly 45–60° from torso rather than flared wide, and do NOT push to true failure. Warm up with: 1×8 empty bar, rest 90s; 1×5 at ~50%, rest 90s; 1×3 at ~70%, rest 2min; 1×1 at ~85%, rest 2min. Then perform a challenging set of 5 with good control. Stop at any shoulder discomfort — note if that happens. Shoulder blades retracted and depressed throughout.
raw_results.record_label: Weight (kg or lb) × reps completed in your best set of 5

raw_results.name: Max Push-Ups to Failure
raw_results.protocol: After at least 3 minutes of rest following bench press. No warm-up set needed — bench press has already primed you. Get into a standard push-up position: hands just outside shoulders, body in a straight line from head to heel. Perform as many reps as possible without stopping, maintaining full range (chest within 2–3 cm of floor, elbows fully extended at top). Stop when you can no longer maintain form. Note: use a neutral wrist position; if wrist discomfort arises, stop early and note it.
raw_results.record_label: Total reps completed with good form

raw_results.name: Forearm Plank Hold
raw_results.protocol: Rest 3 minutes after push-ups. Get into a forearm plank: elbows directly under shoulders, forearms flat, body in a straight line from head to heel. Hold as long as possible while maintaining a truly neutral spine — no sagging hips, no pike. Stop the timer the moment your hips drop more than ~2 cm or you need to adjust position. Have someone time you, or use a phone propped nearby. No specific warm-up needed.

raw_results.name: Deep Bodyweight Squat Hold — Mobility Assessment
raw_results.protocol: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, toes angled out 15–30°. Lower into the deepest squat you can comfortably hold, aiming to get your hips below your knees. Hold for up to 60 seconds. Note: can you hold the bottom position with your heels flat on the floor? Do your knees track over your toes? Is there any rounding in the lower back or discomfort in hips, knees, or ankles? No hands on the floor — arms extended in front for balance if needed. This is an observation test, not a pass/fail.
raw_results.record_label: Describe: heel contact (yes/no), depth achieved, any discomfort or compensation noted
raw_results.result: Yes heel contact, depth achieved, no discomfort

raw_results.name: 20-Minute Steady Pool Swim — Aerobic Baseline
raw_results.protocol: Head to the pool last. Swim any stroke you're comfortable with (freestyle recommended) at a pace you could sustain for the full 20 minutes — conversational effort, roughly 60–70% of max HR (target ~100–115 bpm if you can monitor). Warm up with 2 easy lengths. Then start a 20-minute continuous effort, counting total lengths completed. If you wear your Garmin in the water, record average HR. If not, note your perceived exertion (scale 1–10) at the midpoint and end. Cool down with 2 easy lengths after the timer.
raw_results.record_label: Total lengths in 20 min, average HR if available, and perceived effort (1–10) at midpoint and finish
